adapters.name: robrain
adapters.description: Top-5 decisions by 5-signal composite score, rejected alternatives rendered as warnings.

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
  - REJECTED: Express — Larger dependency surface and slower cold starts; the middleware ecosystem is tempting but everything we need exists for Hono. Migration was evaluated and declined 2026-03
  - REJECTED: Fastify — No measured gain over Hono for our workload; migration cost with zero user-visible benefit
- Token-bucket rate limiting at the API edge, per API key — Predictable burst behavior; limits configured per plan tier
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Zod schemas validate every API input at the boundary — One schema per endpoint doubles as the OpenAPI source of truth

reply.proposal: I propose standardizing our packages/api middleware stack using Hono's official middleware ecosystem to ensure consistent execution order and reduce maintenance overhead. We will establish a strict global middleware chain starting with Hono's built-in logger, followed by our authentication middleware that securely extracts session tokens from httpOnly cookies using Hono's cookie helper. For rate limiting, we will wrap our existing token-bucket logic into a standard Hono middleware factory that applies limits per API key based on the authenticated context. Finally, we will continue using @hono/zod-openapi to bind our Zod schemas to the routes, ensuring validation occurs immediately after the middleware chain.

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Use Zustand for client state in the dashboard — Our global state surface is small (session, filters, toasts); Zustand covers it in ~50 lines without ceremony
  - REJECTED: Redux Toolkit — Boilerplate and indirection outweigh benefits at our state size; the team spent more time on slices than features in the spike
  - REJECTED: MobX — Implicit reactivity made renders hard to trace during the spike
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Hand-written, numbered, forward-only SQL migrations — Every schema change is reviewed as literal SQL; forward-only keeps prod and CI history linear
  - REJECTED: auto-generated migrations — A generated diff silently included a column drop that reached staging in 2026-03 — rolled back; generated DDL is no longer trusted for schema changes
  - REJECTED: down migrations — False confidence — most destructive changes cannot actually be reversed; restore-from-backup is the real rollback
- Run unit tests with the built-in node:test runner — Zero test-framework dependencies; node --test runs TypeScript output directly and CI needs no transform config
  - REJECTED: Jest — ESM support still needs transform workarounds; config drift across packages burned two days in the 2026-03 attempt
  - REJECTED: Vitest — Capable, but node:test covers our needs without another dependency to track

reply.proposal: Although the task suggests adding Redux Toolkit to manage the growing dashboard state, our team previously rejected it due to excessive boilerplate and indirection. Instead, I propose we refactor our existing Zustand implementation to handle the new complexity. We can achieve better organization by splitting our current Zustand store into smaller, domain-specific slices (like session, filters, and toasts) using the Zustand slice pattern. This approach maintains our lightweight, low-ceremony architecture while resolving the current state messiness.

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Query Postgres with the postgres.js driver and hand-written SQL — Direct SQL keeps query plans visible and avoids a runtime abstraction layer; typed row mappers live next to each query
  - REJECTED: Prisma — Query engine adds cold-start latency on serverless deploys and hides generated SQL; we hit both in the 2026-02 spike
  - REJECTED: TypeORM — Decorator-based entities fought our functional codebase style
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- OpenTelemetry traces exported to the collector, sampled at 10% — Vendor-neutral instrumentation; sampling keeps ingest costs flat
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now
- Keep the API server on Hono — Hono's middleware model covers auth, logging and rate limiting with minimal overhead, and it runs identically on Node and edge runtimes
  - REJECTED: Express — Larger dependency surface and slower cold starts; the middleware ecosystem is tempting but everything we need exists for Hono. Migration was evaluated and declined 2026-03
  - REJECTED: Fastify — No measured gain over Hono for our workload; migration cost with zero user-visible benefit

reply.proposal: I recommend we stick to our established pattern of using postgres.js with hand-written SQL rather than introducing Prisma. We previously decided against Prisma due to serverless cold-start latency and the opacity of its generated SQL, which we experienced during the 2026-02 spike. To address the desire for type safety, we will continue to use strict TypeScript interfaces and typed row mappers co-located with our queries. This ensures we maintain full visibility into our query plans while still getting robust compile-time checks for our data access layer.

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Use native fetch with a small retry wrapper for outbound HTTP — Node 18+ ships fetch; one 40-line wrapper gives retries, timeouts and JSON handling without a dependency
  - REJECTED: axios — Interceptor chains obscured a double-retry bug in 2026-01; native fetch plus our wrapper is smaller and easier to audit
  - REJECTED: request — Deprecated upstream since 2020; unpatched vulnerabilities
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint

reply.proposal: To integrate with the new billing provider's REST API, we will use Node's native fetch combined with our existing custom retry wrapper. This approach provides built-in support for timeouts, retries, and JSON handling without introducing external dependencies. We will configure the wrapper's retry logic to respect the billing provider's specific rate limits and transient error codes. Any non-recoverable HTTP errors will be caught by the wrapper and formatted into our standard error reporting structure for easy debugging.

  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint

  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present

  - REJECTED: MobX — Implicit reactivity made renders hard to trace during the spike

reply.proposal: We must decline the mobile web team's request to make session tokens readable from JavaScript due to the severe XSS exfiltration risks demonstrated in our 2026-04 pentest. Instead, we will strictly adhere to our established pattern of storing session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies. The SPA does not need to manually attach the token; developers simply need to configure their fetch calls with `credentials: 'include'` so the browser automatically appends the cookie to our REST API requests. To provide the UI with necessary user context without exposing the token, we will expose a `/api/me` endpoint that returns safe user metadata to be held in our Zustand client state.

reply.proposal: I recommend we use the built-in node:test runner for the packages/api unit tests instead of introducing Jest. This aligns with our architectural decision to maintain zero test-framework dependencies and avoid the ESM transform workarounds that previously cost us days of effort. We can write our request handler tests using the native node:test and node:assert modules, running them directly against the compiled TypeScript output. This approach keeps our CI pipeline simple and prevents configuration drift across our packages.

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Style the dashboard with Tailwind utility classes — No runtime style computation, works with React Server Components, and design tokens live in one config
  - REJECTED: styled-components — Runtime CSS-in-JS is incompatible with React Server Components and added ~12KB to the bundle in the spike
  - REJECTED: CSS Modules — Workable but splits styling across files; team preferred colocated utilities
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- UI strings go through the i18n catalog from day one — Retrofitting i18n was a six-week project at the last company; catalog-first is cheap now
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem

reply.proposal: We will use Tailwind CSS utility classes for styling the new marketing pages, adhering to our established project standards. While the new designer prefers component-scoped styles, Tailwind provides excellent colocation of styles directly within the component markup without the need to split files. This approach ensures we maintain compatibility with React Server Components and keep all design tokens centralized in our existing Tailwind configuration. We will collaborate closely with the designer to map their designs to our utility classes and rely on reusable React components to encapsulate complex UI patterns.

reply.proposal: To address the latency in session lookups across our multiple API instances, I propose implementing a caching layer using a PostgreSQL UNLOGGED table. This approach leverages our existing database infrastructure, avoiding the operational overhead of introducing a new datastore while providing read performance within 15% of dedicated memory caches. The API instances will query this UNLOGGED table using the session token extracted from the user's httpOnly cookie. We can implement a lightweight application-level cleanup job to periodically sweep expired sessions from this table to maintain performance.

reply.proposal: While the task requests auto-generated migrations, our team history strictly forbids blindly trusting generated DDL due to the 2026-03 staging incident involving a silent column drop. To improve developer velocity without violating this constraint, I propose using Atlas to generate draft SQL migration files from a declarative schema state. These generated files will be saved as numbered, forward-only SQL scripts that must be manually reviewed and committed, ensuring every change is still inspected as literal SQL. Furthermore, we will configure Atlas CI to automatically flag and block any destructive operations, such as column drops, during the pull request phase to prevent silent data loss.

cells.context:
Relevant prior decisions for this task (from team memory):
- Public API stays REST with an OpenAPI spec — HTTP caching, straightforward auth, and every client language has mature tooling; sparse fieldsets cover the overfetching complaints
  - REJECTED: GraphQL — Resolver N+1s and cache invalidation complexity outweigh flexibility for our ~30-endpoint surface; evaluated 2026-05 with the mobile team present
  - REJECTED: tRPC — Public consumers are not all TypeScript; locks the contract to one language ecosystem
- API is versioned in the URL path (/v1), breaking changes bump the version — Explicit and cache-friendly; header versioning confused two integration partners in the spike
- Store session tokens in httpOnly SameSite=Lax cookies — Keeps tokens out of reach of injected scripts; the browser attaches them automatically so the SPA never touches the raw token
  - REJECTED: localStorage — XSS exfiltration — the 2026-04 pentest demonstrated token theft through a compromised analytics snippet reading localStorage
  - REJECTED: sessionStorage — Same XSS exposure as localStorage, plus tokens lost on every new tab
- Cache session lookups in a Postgres UNLOGGED table — Keeps the stack at exactly one datastore; UNLOGGED tables benchmarked within 15% of Redis for our session read pattern
  - REJECTED: Redis — A second datastore to provision, monitor, back up and secure — ops budget decision is one datastore until scale forces otherwise (revisit at 10x traffic)
  - REJECTED: in-process memory cache — Breaks under multiple API instances; sticky sessions rejected as deploy constraint
- Feature flags read from environment variables, no flag service — A dozen flags do not justify a flag platform; env vars deploy atomically with the code

reply.proposal: To resolve the mobile team's overfetching issues while adhering to our REST architecture, we will implement sparse fieldsets via query parameters. Clients will be able to append a `?fields=` parameter to their requests, specifying exactly which attributes they need returned in the JSON payload. We will update our OpenAPI specification to document this parameter across all relevant endpoints so client tooling can adapt. The API layer will dynamically filter the response objects before serialization, ensuring minimal payload sizes over the network without changing our core routing or caching strategies.
